channel.title: Psychology Today – Latest
channel.description: Latest articles from Psychology Today (full content)

item.title: Building a Therapeutic Revolution: Veterans Lead the Way
item.description:
Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder

Building a Therapeutic Revolution: Veterans Lead the Way

How veteran care is shaping the future of mental health.

Posted March 17, 2026

Key points
  • The therapeutic alliance improves outcomes and extends beyond the clinician–patient relationship.
  • Veterans face high mental health burden, with suicide prevention a top national clinical priority.
  • Bipartisan efforts are expanding rigorous research into emerging therapies within VA systems.
  • Coordinated institutional alignment may be as critical as new treatments in advancing care.

Much of contemporary discussion in mental health innovation centers on treatments—mechanisms of action, clinical endpoints, and regulatory milestones. Yet effective care depends not only on interventions, but on the relational and institutional structures that enable them. One of medicine’s most established constructs addressing this dimension is the therapeutic alliance.

The Clinical Foundations of Alliance

The therapeutic alliance originated in psychotherapy as a framework describing the collaborative bond between clinician and patient. Decades of empirical research have demonstrated that alliance quality correlates with treatment adherence, engagement, and outcomes across psychiatric conditions.

The American Medical Association formalized this concept within its Code of Medical Ethics, defining the patient-physician relationship as a “collaborative effort” grounded in a “mutually respectful alliance,” and emphasizing that “the practice of medicine is fundamentally a moral activity.”

Traditionally, this alliance has been examined within the boundaries of the clinic. But the principle underlying it—structured collaboration in service of patient outcomes—has implications that extend beyond the dyadic clinical encounter.

A Systems-Level Mental Health Challenge

Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), major depressive disorder, substance use disorders, and traumatic brain injury remain among the most complex and high-burden psychiatric conditions. Remission rates remain suboptimal in treatment-resistant populations, and relapse is common.

For U.S. veterans, the urgency is particularly acute.

The Department of Veterans Affairs’ National Veteran Suicide Prevention Annual Report states unequivocally: “Reducing Veteran suicide is the Department of Veterans Affairs' (VA’s) top clinical priority.” Veterans experience elevated rates of PTSD, alcohol use disorder, and depression compared with the general population. Peer-reviewed data published in The American Journal of Managed Care indicate that approximately one in three veterans is diagnosed with at least one mental health disorder, and 41 percent receive a diagnosis related to mental health or behavioral adjustment.

These epidemiologic realities underscore the need not only for therapeutic innovation, but for coordinated institutional response.

Bipartisan Support for Expanding Clinical Research

In this context, bipartisan legislation in the United States Congress has focused on expanding structured research into emerging therapies for veterans, including psychedelic-assisted treatments currently under investigation in FDA-regulated trials.

Bipartisan leadership of the Congressional PATH (Psychedelics Advancing Therapies) Caucus has been instrumental in expanding psychedelic-assisted treatments. Their legislative efforts aim to expand VA participation in controlled clinical trials, establish investigational research programs, create extended access pathways where appropriate, and develop VA Centers of Excellence dedicated to evaluating innovative psychiatric treatments.

These proposals do not alter FDA evidentiary standards. Rather, they seek to increase institutional capacity to conduct rigorous research within established regulatory frameworks. The significance lies less in political symbolism than in structural alignment: federal agencies, research institutions, and healthcare systems coordinating to generate high-quality evidence.

Institutional Engagement Within the VA

The Veterans Health Administration is the largest integrated healthcare system in the United States, serving approximately 9 million patients across more than 170 medical centers and over 1,000 outpatient sites. Its scale and centralized structure make it uniquely positioned to conduct multi-site clinical trials and longitudinal outcome tracking within a unified system.

Secretary of Veterans Affairs Doug Collins has publicly acknowledged the severity of the crisis, stating that “Veteran suicide has been a scourge on our nation for far too long.” In a notable public moment, he became the first member of an administration to speak from the White House about the potential of psychedelic medicine, signaling openness to evaluating emerging therapies within a research-based framework.

The VA is currently collaborating on multiple psychedelic medicine trials, including studies conducted at VA health centers. As with other investigational psychiatric treatments, these studies proceed through phased clinical development designed to evaluate safety, dosing, efficacy, durability, and risk.

The scientific standards remain consistent. What has evolved is the degree of institutional engagement.

A Broader Blueprint for Mental Health Innovation

Melissa Lavasani, CEO of the nonprofit Psychedelic Medicine Coalition, has described this moment as a “mindshift in the future of mental health in America.” She notes that the Veterans Health Administration—serving 9 million patients with a workforce of approximately 400,000 across hundreds of facilities—represents more than a policy environment. In her words, the current effort is “more than policy proposals— it’s a blueprint for a new era of mental health care in the United States.”

Her observation reflects a broader implementation science principle: Large integrated systems often function as translational accelerators. When research infrastructure, regulatory clarity, and healthcare delivery operate in alignment, innovation can move more efficiently from controlled trials into real-world care settings.

From Dyadic Alliance to Institutional Alignment

The therapeutic alliance literature consistently demonstrates that alignment between clinician and patient improves measurable outcomes. At a systems level, similar alignment between institutions may influence how effectively new treatments are evaluated and, if validated, implemented.

In translational psychiatry, fragmentation can slow progress. Research findings may not be integrated into care models. Funding uncertainty can disrupt long-term study design. Political polarization can impede sustained collaboration.

When policymakers collaborate across party lines to support structured evidence generation, the result is not merely legislative movement. It is increased predictability in funding, clearer research pathways, and stronger infrastructure for clinical investigation.

In this sense, extending the therapeutic alliance beyond the therapy room is not metaphorical. It is operational.

Mental health innovation will ultimately be judged by data—by safety profiles, effect sizes, durability of response, and real-world effectiveness. Emerging therapies, including psychedelic-assisted treatments, must meet established scientific standards before widespread adoption.

But generating that data depends on coordinated institutional engagement.

The original therapeutic alliance described a shared commitment between clinician and patient. Applied more broadly, the same principle suggests that durable progress in mental health requires alignment among researchers, regulators, policymakers, and healthcare systems.

For veterans facing elevated rates of suicide and complex psychiatric comorbidities, such alignment may prove as essential as any single therapeutic advance.

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Adi Zuloff-Shani Ph.D.
